headline_summary: Reuters (Apr 8): U.S., Iran and Israel announce a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan; talks planned to pursue longer-term settlement.
Reuters (Apr 8): Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without a permit; shipowners and major liners seek clarity before resuming transits.
Reuters (Apr 7-8): Maritime risk remains elevated with ~30 incidents since Feb 28; insurers and shipping groups warn of continued targeting risk absent coordination.
Reuters (Apr 4): Drone strike sparks fire at foreign oil company storage units in Barjasiyah, west of Basra, highlighting spillover risk to Iraqi oil logistics.
Market focus: Even with ceasefire optics, permit-based passage keeps physical supply normalization slow; war-risk premia in freight/insurance and oil volatility persist.
risk_drivers: Permit-based Strait of Hormuz access (risk of selective enforcement and sudden re-tightening)
Ceasefire is time-limited and implementation-dependent; negotiation gaps can re-ignite escalation
High maritime incident count and explicit threats against unpermitted vessels sustain war-risk premia
Spillover attacks on Iraqi energy storage/logistics demonstrate regionalization risk

Energy: oil_up_risk: Permit-gated Hormuz transit keeps upside skew in crude and refined products; physical flows normalize slowly.
Freight/insurance: War-risk insurance and freight rates likely remain elevated until routine, unthreatened passage resumes.
Gas/LNG: Any continued Gulf chokepoint uncertainty supports LNG volatility via shipping/insurance constraints.
FX: em_fx_pressure: Higher/volatile energy prices can pressure oil-importer EM FX via trade balances and inflation.
risk_off_flows: Escalation headlines favor USD/CHF/JPY and reduce carry appetite.
Rates: rates_credit_spreads: Risk-off and energy-driven inflation uncertainty can widen credit spreads and lift breakevens.
Policy uncertainty: Central banks may face worse inflation-growth tradeoffs if energy shock persists.
Equities: risk_off_flows: Airlines, transport, and energy-intensive industries face margin risk; energy/defense may outperform on shock headlines.
Regional risk: Iraq/GCC equities can see volatility—benefit from higher oil but face operational/security tail-risk.

sources.title: What the US, Iran, Israel and Pakistan have said about the ceasefire
Shippers seek clarity on Hormuz passage as Iran issues fresh warnings
Firefighters stop fire at drone-hit foreign oil storage units in Basra

events.title: U.S., Iran and Israel announce a two-week ceasefire brokered by Pakistan; talks planned to pursue longer-term settlement
events.implication: Reduces near-term escalation risk but remains time-limited; risk premia can re-price quickly if compliance falters or talks stall.

events.title: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shipping majors seek clarity before resuming transits
events.implication: Permit-based passage keeps shipping flows constrained and supports persistent war-risk premia in freight/insurance; oil/LNG supply timing remains uncertain.

events.title: Major shipping companies remain cautious on Hormuz despite ceasefire; Hapag-Lloyd cites 6-8 weeks to restore normal flows
events.implication: Extended recovery window implies continued physical tightness and higher forward volatility for energy and shipping-sensitive assets.

events.title: Insurers and shipping groups warn maritime risk remains elevated after ~30 incidents since Feb. 28 amid Gulf conflict
events.implication: Higher war-risk insurance and routing constraints raise effective delivered energy costs and reinforce risk-off positioning.

events.title: Drone strike hits foreign oil company storage units at Barjasiyah (west of Basra); firefighters stop blaze
events.implication: Signals spillover to Iraqi storage/logistics nodes; raises risk premium on regional supply reliability even outside Iran.

events.title: Selective Hormuz passage policy emerges (permit-gated transits) rather than full reopening, limiting near-term throughput
events.location_name: Strait of Hormuz, near United Arab Emirates
events.implication: If passage remains discretionary, markets may price a persistent chokepoint option value even under ceasefire headlines.

links.to: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shipping majors seek clarity before resuming transits

links.from: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shipping majors seek clarity before resuming transits
links.why: Permit-gated transit constrains physical flows and sustains upside tail-risk in crude/prices.

links.from: Insurers and shipping groups warn maritime risk remains elevated after ~30 incidents since Feb. 28 amid Gulf conflict
links.why: Higher war-risk costs and uncertainty can tighten financial conditions and widen credit risk premia during risk-off periods.

links.why: Spillover attacks on Iraqi oil logistics increase perceived fragility of regional supply chain.

links.from: Iran warns Strait of Hormuz remains closed to vessels without permits; shipping majors seek clarity before resuming transits
links.why: Chokepoint uncertainty and explicit targeting threats typically support defensive positioning across assets.
Higher energy prices worsen trade balances for importers, pressuring EM FX and inflation expectations.
